$comment: Committed template. Copy to scenarios.json (also committed) and edit for your real pages. Each scenario is one measurable page. The skill reads scenarios.json.

app.$comment_app: How the skill launches/reaches the app. runCommand is started if the app isn't already serving; baseUrl is where it ends up. Leave runCommand empty to measure an already-running server or a deployed baseUrl.
app.$comment_serve: MEASURE THE REAL PRODUCTION BUNDLE. Build, then static-serve with a gzip-capable server. Avoid webpack-dev-server (`webpack serve`) for weight: it injects ~56KB of HMR client into the bundle even in production mode, tripling the over-wire size. `npx serve` gzips like a real CDN; zero-dep fallback is `cd dist && python3 -m http.server <port>` (no gzip).
app.runCommand: npm run build && npx --yes serve -l 3000 dist

defaults.$comment_defaults: Applied to every scenario unless overridden. cpuThrottle 6x and 5 runs (drop first) are the team baseline — keep them so rows are comparable.

scenarios.name: home
scenarios.$comment_name: Friendly name shown in the results doc.
scenarios.$comment_readySignal: THE most important field. The moment the real content the user came for is on screen — NOT the load event. Use a selector that only exists once data is rendered. Convention: add `data-perf-ready` to your content root once your data has rendered, then this selector matches. Replace with e.g. 'table tbody tr' for a data table.
scenarios.$comment_dataItemCountSelector: Counts rendered items so a 50-row vs 2000-row page aren't compared as equals. e.g. 'table tbody tr'.
scenarios.$comment_dataRequestUrlPattern: JS RegExp (as a string) matching your data requests, used to time the data layer. GraphQL default matches a /graphql endpoint; widen to '/(graphql|api)/' if you also use REST.
